figures.caption: Table 1 The normalized perplexity on the valid corpus of CulturaX. The perplexity is normalized to the vocabulary of Pythia following Wei et al. (2023). “High”, “Medium”, and “Low” indicates the available amount of linguistic resources.

Table 5 The benchmark results of replacing different tokenizers using TokAlign++. The overlapping ratios between the vocabulary of Pythia and other models are 6.23% (Gemma), 26.92% (Qwen2), 28.10% (LLaMA2), and 32.85% (LLaMA3).
Figure 11 The statistic of parameter deviation in Pythia models after vocabulary adaptation.

Figure 1 Motivation of TokAlign++. (a) When large language models learn new languages, the length of tokens and inference time cost often increase for the lower compression rate of vanilla Tokenzier on the new language. (b) Many large language models have their own tokenizer with different sizes. The compression rates of code and low-resource language texts often lag behind other domains.

Figure 3 Illustration of TokAlign++ to align token IDs from different vocabularies. We train token representations on the tokenized corpus or obtain the token representations from the source large language model (u1 and u2 steps), and align token IDs using the bilingual lexicon induction method VecMap. It is noted that the IDs of tokens belonging to both vocabularies are directly replaced without alignment.
Table 4 The main results of replacing the vocabulary of Pythia or LLaMA3 to Gemma. The best performance among the eight methods is displayed in bold.
Figure 2 TokAlign++ aims to bridge these corpora through aligning the similar token ID pairs.

Table 6 The benchmark results of replacing Gemma Tokenizer for Pythia1B using TokAlign++ without any tuning under different settings. ∗ denotes the cross-scale generalization experiment, where the hidden states are extracted from Pythia6.9B.
Table A.1 The compression rates (bytes/token) of different tokenizers.
Figure 9 Influence of the percentage of embedding only tuning stage in the vocabulary adaptation process.
Figure 10 The loss curve of Pythia1B under two-stage tuning or direct full parameters tuning.
Figure 8 The average vocabulary coverage (a) and initial training loss of Pythia1B (b) when different amounts of tokens are used to train the GloVe vector.
Table 2 Zero-shot in-context learning results of cross-lingual transfer.
Table B.1 Details of language codes in this work.

Table 7 The case study of new Gemma tokens from six languages in the target vocabulary with top-3 source tokens aligned. The language families of English, Chinese, and Korean are Indo-European, Sino-Tibetan, and Koreanic, respectively.
Figure 7 The relationship between initial training loss and BLEU-1 (a, c) or BERTScore (b, d) for Pythia1B.
